questions.text: Why does converting a high-cardinality string column (where almost all rows have unique strings) to the category type increase memory usage instead of reducing it?
options: Category types automatically convert strings to float matrices, increasing memory.
The category type creates a lookup index of all unique values. If almost all rows are unique, the index overhead plus the integer code storage exceeds the original string storage.
NumPy memory blocks do not support high-cardinality categories.
Pandas category types compress values by replicating them, which fails under high cardinality.
questions.correct_answer: The category type creates a lookup index of all unique values. If almost all rows are unique, the index overhead plus the integer code storage exceeds the original string storage.
questions.explanation: The category type encodes strings as integer codes (e.g., int8) and maintains a lookup index mapping those codes back to unique string values. If the cardinality is extremely high, the overhead of storing the lookup mapping index in addition to the integer code array exceeds the memory required for raw string pointers, making it counter-productive.

questions.text: Which parameter configuration allows pd.read_csv to process a 100 GB file without exceeding system memory limits?
options: chunksize=N (where N is the number of rows per block)
questions.correct_answer: chunksize=N (where N is the number of rows per block)
questions.explanation: Defining the chunksize parameter makes pd.read_csv return a TextFileReader generator instead of loading the entire dataset into memory. This streams the file in blocks of size N, keeping the memory utilization restricted to the size of a single chunk.
